behaviors.description: Only files changed in the pull request are analyzed — unchanged files are not scanned
behaviors.justification: Must verify that PR file detection (via Git diff or GitHub API) works correctly and filtering excludes non-changed files. Mocking the file-list detection would hide failures in the actual Git or GitHub integration. Requires real filesystem state or GitHub PR context to detect changes.
behaviors.test_description: Create a local Git repository with 10 files. Stage, commit, and designate them as baseline. Modify exactly 3 of those files. Run the linter in a mock PR context where changed files = [file1, file3, file7]. Verify: (1) analysis results mention exactly 3 files, (2) the 7 unchanged files do not appear in results or logs, (3) analysis skipped scanning the unchanged files.

behaviors.description: Findings are grouped by severity (error, warning, info) in the posted PR comment
behaviors.justification: Grouping is pure data transformation with no I/O: findings-with-severities → grouped findings. Can test by mocking linter output and mock GitHub API calls, verifying the comment body contains the correct structure. Real failure would be incorrect grouping (mixed severities, wrong order, lost findings), all detectable in output structure without real linter or real GitHub.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test set of findings: 5 errors, 3 warnings, 2 info-level messages. Pass through the grouping-and-formatting function. Verify: (1) comment body has three sections in order [errors, warnings, info], (2) all 5 errors appear in errors section, (3) all 3 warnings in warnings section, (4) all 2 info in info section, (5) within each section, order matches input order, (6) no findings are lost or duplicated across sections.

behaviors.description: A pull request that touches 200 files completes analysis within the 10-minute Actions timeout
behaviors.justification: Performance behavior requires real file I/O (200 files on disk), real linter subprocess execution, and actual timing measurement. Mocking the linter would hide timeout failures entirely—the mock returns instantly, so the test would pass even if the real linter times out. Must run against realistic 200-file workload with real subprocess to catch performance regressions.
behaviors.test_description: Generate or load 200 files with realistic code (mix of sizes, LOC, complexity). Run the actual linter subprocess on all 200 files. Use system timer to measure wall-clock elapsed time from start to final result posting. Verify: (1) total elapsed time < 600 seconds, (2) all 200 files appear in analysis results, (3) no files were skipped or timed out individually, (4) subprocess completed successfully with non-zero exit only on actual errors, not timeouts.

behaviors.description: When the linter process crashes on a malformed file, the remaining files are still analyzed and results posted
behaviors.justification: Behavior depends on subprocess-level crash handling: a real linter process must fail, and the orchestrator must recover and continue. Mocking the linter or mocking the crash would skip the actual error handling path. Real failure: subprocess crashes → orchestrator stops → remaining files are not analyzed. Requires real subprocess interaction and error handling in the actual execution environment.
behaviors.test_description: Create a set of 10 files. One file is intentionally malformed (syntax that causes linter to crash; e.g., binary garbage). Run the linter as a real subprocess on all 10 files. Verify: (1) subprocess exits with non-zero code or crash signal, (2) the 9 well-formed files are still analyzed and findings appear in results, (3) the malformed file is logged with an error message, (4) final results include data from the 9 good files, (5) analysis did not halt—comment was still posted to PR.

behaviors.description: Duplicate findings on the same line from overlapping rules are deduplicated in the output
behaviors.justification: Deduplication is pure data-structure logic: takes findings with potential duplicates (same file, line, rule) and removes duplicates. No I/O, no external dependencies. Can be tested by constructing mock findings with known duplicates and verifying output. Real failure would be duplicates in the output, detectable by comparing input and output structures without running real rules or linter.
behaviors.test_description: Create a findings list where two different rule engines flag the same line of the same file with identical findings (same message, same severity). Include 10 total findings: 3 unique, 2 pairs of duplicates (4 duplicate entries), and 3 more unique. Pass through deduplication function. Verify: (1) duplicates are removed, leaving 8 findings total, (2) for each duplicate pair, exactly one copy is retained, (3) non-duplicate findings are all present, (4) findings on different lines are not affected by dedup.
